speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This second interview with Clara Payne Grove took place at her home in Moscow on November 7th, 1975. The interviewer is Sam Schrager.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What year were you born in?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: 1879, May 13th. And I never have attributed any of the odds and ends to the fact that it was the 13th.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were your parents homesteaded at all? Were they homesteading folks? How did they come to be in Iowa?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: I come off pioneer stock as far back as I know anything about my family line. One pioneer was a nice young, big strapping young Irish lad of 16 who had gone to the seaside to see the wonder of the boats, the ships. That was undoubtedly somewhere in 1700. And a sailor approached him as he was on the wharf, staring at the boats, and asking if he'd like to see the inside of a boat. One boy wouldn't. So they went on board. And the sailor showed him all about the deck. And he said, now let's go below deck and see what's down there.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: So they went below deck, and they came to one door that was shut, and the boy said, what's in here?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And the sailor said, go in and see. So he opened the door and let the boy in ahead of him and pulled the door shut and bolted it. And it wasn't unbolted until the ship was so far at sea there was no possibility of anybody swimming ashore. And he was put on deck with the hands, and lived with the hands. And all the while, this was summertime, all the while the crew slept on deck. They just lay down on the deck and slept. Well, when they got to this side of the Atlantic, the boy knew about the plantation owners indenturing a man. And indenturing means that they took him as a slave until he was 21. At that time, the plantation owner was supposed to give him a horse, bridle and saddle, and sometimes he did, and sometimes he didn't, as he chose. But being indentured was enough of a slave that anyone attempting to run away was fair game for anyone who shot him down. That was not murder. That was merely capturing a slave. But anyway, that's the Irish line, which I always claim was my nationality. I have a mixture also of Swiss Dutch and of English, and in late years I found out there was Scotch mixed in it. I hadn't known that until later years. But on the other side, back in the 1700s, an ancestor on the Swiss Dutch side in the forest around his house cut down such an enormous black walnut tree that he made a chest out of the boards that he made.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And the boards were so large that the chest, which stood about two feet high, had no piecing in it. The cover, the bottom, the ends, and the sides, each was one board. No metal at all fastened it together. It was dovetailed together. And dovetailed so expertly and exactly that to this day, that chest, I don't have it, but my daughter does have it. To this day, that chest can be pulled apart and put back dovetailed together. There was that ancestry, and he made it as a wedding chest for his daughter. And immediately after the marriage ceremony, the daughter and her nice new husband, with the chest in his two -wheeled, one ox cart, and they, walking beside, started west from, now I'm not sure whether it was from Carolina or from Virginia. At any rate, it was back in 1700, before either state was divided. They started west, and one place they came to, the forest was so dense that they couldn't get through just as they were. So they took the chest off of the cart and took the ox loose from it and led the locked ox through for what trail that would make. Then they turned the cart up on one wheel and carried the cart through. Then they carried the chest through and put it back in the cart and went on west. They came to another place that they couldn't get the chest through, so they pulled the dovetailing apart and laid the pieces and the contents out on the ground and led the ox through for what trail that would make. And then they went back and picked the chest up piece by piece and carried piece by piece through. But still they went west. We have the word of them in Indiana, where my mother and 11 other children of the same family were born. And then from there they went to Minnesota.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: They were there at a time of Indian War, but they were never disturbed. At one time, perhaps not during the war time, but at one time, an extremely cold winter morning, Grandfather got up and built the kitchen fire and put the tea kettle on with what water was in it to get it boiling to thaw the pump so he could get a pail of water. So when the tea kettle boiled, he took it out to the pump and just at that time, an Indian came along, just one Indian alone. He was dressed solely in his breechcloth, his moccasins, and a blanket. And he asked if he might have a drink of water. Grandfather told him yes, as soon as he could get the pump thawed so he could get water. So he went on and used what water he had to thaw the pump and got water.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And he said to the Indian, aren't you cold? And the Indian said, your face cold?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Grandfather said, no, my face doesn't get cold. Indian all face.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And it may have been at that special time or some other time, Grandmother was having a rag carpet woven. That was what you did for a carpet in those days. You saved everything you could to sew into carpet rags and then you got the person who had the loom to weave your carpet. Well, Grandmother was having this carpet woven. And she said to Grandfather one day to saddle a horse for her, she wanted to ride over to the woman who had the loom and tell her exactly how to stripe the colors. And Grandfather said, no, the Indians were out. She couldn't go. That he would go. And Grandmother said, no, you'd forget before you got there how I wanted striped and I want it like I want it and I'm going. You saddle old dolly for me. She's afraid of Indians and I'll know if there are any about. So Grandfather saddled old dolly and Grandmother mounted on the side saddle. And when she got home, she said she knew that in a certain clump of bushes beside the road, there must have been an Indian because old dolly just refused to pass it. And she had quite a time to make dolly past that clump of bushes so she knew there was an Indian in it. But that was nothing compared to striping a carpet. That side saddle was in our family until, oh, it's been more than 40 years ago. My sister who had the side saddle died. The children of her husband by his former marriage swarmed in and took what they wanted and burned what they didn't want. Whether they burned the side saddle or took it, we never knew. That was the last of the side saddle. Well, I came to Idaho. I came to Troy. The reason was that the home of myself and my daughter, which was my family, lived in Moore, Montana. And we had come west, pioneer stop, didn't know anything, any of us, except to go west. We came west to the home of a brother at Ruby, Washington, which is north of Spokane, north of Newport, Washington. And he was away. We lived in his home for a time. And back in Moore, Montana, there had been a weekly newspaper that was edited by a man named Wilbur Johns, J -O -H -N -S, Johns. And my daughter had stuck type a little for him in the office. And I had been his local correspondent for news. And I wrote to Mr. Johns at Moore, where I still owned a house, to know if he would have work on the paper for me if I returned to my home in Moore. In the meantime, unknown to me, Mr. Johns had come to Troy, Idaho, and taken over the weekly newspaper there. And he answered me from Troy, saying that if I wanted to come to Troy, he would give me half -day work in the office. So, daughter and I, after we had been as far west as the land goes, which is Silverdale, across a narrow neck of land from Seattle out in the Pacific. And we had been at Silverdale, keeping house there for some people for a time. And then we went to Troy for me to take this half -day position in the weekly with Mr. Johns.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Did Mr.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Johns ever tell you why he came to Troy and left?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: No, he never did. I never asked him and he never told me why he went to Troy. But I think it probably was because the little town of Moore had mostly dried up and blown away. And times were hard there, and I imagine that he was not making a living off of his paper. So he just discontinued it and went to Troy where the pickings were a little better. I'm sure that was why. So, I worked half -day, and in Troy, the women school teachers, secretaries, and the store clerk got together and rented a furnished house and lived there wanting a housekeeper. So my daughter and I went there. She was in high school.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And the next year graduated from the Troy High School in 1927. We were in the house with the girls for a little while. Well, that just didn't do very well because the child needed a home, which you don't get in one little upstairs room that isn't heated, nor in the kitchen. So I got the loan of a cookhouse for a threshing crew that the man very kindly hauled up into the yard of the house where I was housekeeping. And my daughter and I lived in there and kept the house and did the cooking and so on for the girls. And we did that for the remainder of the year.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was your daughter able to help with the housework very much while she was going to school?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Oh, she did help some, but it wasn't difficult because the cooking was practically the only part of it. And that was nothing to cook for half a dozen people. She did help, yes, but there just wasn't so much to do. It didn't bother me any at all to put in my half day at the office.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: So she was busy with her high school and I was busy with the office. And in our second year, one day Mr. Johns and I had been sticking type and we both met at the stone to empty our sticks at the same moment. And as we emptied them, Mr.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Johns looked up at me and he said, do you want to buy this paper?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Well, that was the first I'd ever heard of such a thing, but immediately, just as though we'd been talking about the weather, I said, yes, I do. He said, well, let's go to the bank and make out the papers. I said, all right, let's go. So we did. And as they were making out the papers, the banker,

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: oh, you know his name, Oly Bowman, I said, and you will pay this amount every month. I said, well, make the contract that I will pay that amount and may pay as much more as I please. And he said, oh, you won't need that. I said, well, put it in the contract anyway. I said, put it in. Well, he and Mr. Johns exchanged a little look between them of real amusement. But anyway, he put it in. There wasn't one payment, but I made the payment and a little more. Because people seeing it under new hands formed in to hand their business to the Troy News, which was known as the Troy News since, of course. But any rag has its own pet name. So we were working on that. One day a man came in and offered me $500 for the plant and the building of everything, lock, stock and type. I told him no. I wasn't interested.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: But I did begin to try to sell. And right out of the blue there came a man who at a former time had had the same paper and given it up and gone elsewhere and wanted to come back. And he came in and he offered me much more than the place was worth. It was said, for one thing, that the press was the one that Mark Twain had brought across the plains with them. And I always said, that's pure nonsense. Mark Twain never brought anything as useless as that old press across the plains. So I don't know whether or not it is the press that he brought. It was a huge thing. It would practically have filled that alcove. A huge thing. And I stood on a box this high to feed it by hand. But anyway, we put out the paper and Mr. Johns, as he was leaving, he said, now here's some boilerplate you're going to need when you have a vacant spot and nothing to put in it. I said, alright. Well, instead of having ever a vacant spot, I'd had it only just a little while until I had to put out a supplement to have room to put all the news in. And I did really love that. Tell me how it changed under your hands that it got to be so much bigger and that it got more business.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What is it that you did that made you have to put out a supplement?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Well, people were bringing in news. Nobody could go to Moscow for a day of trading. Nobody could go to Spokane to spend an afternoon. Nobody could do anything. But everybody brought me the news. And when you have a town newspaper, what the people want and demand and frequently can't get is whether Mr. and Mrs. John Smith spent the day in Spokane or whether Lillian Smith took a prize at school. Every little thing that happens is what they want to know. And those were the things that people were just pouring into the office. And I was printing all of it because it's what they wanted. And if they wanted it, I wanted them to have it. So that is how it happened that I had to put out a supplement. And as for knowing anything about owning a paper or the legal aspects that are necessary to be conformed with or how to feed the press or much of anything else, I didn't know anything about it. Not anything. I knew how to be a good correspondent for news, yes. But it takes a little more than that to put a paper in the post office.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have to learn the rest or did you have help for the rest?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: I had a woman there that Mr. Johns had wanted me to put on for help. She didn't know as much about it as I did. And she was just a real nuisance. And I found that she had come because it was supposed that after just a little, her husband would get the paper for practically nothing. And she'd already be there in the office. So I just kept her a little while and she was highly indignant when I told her I couldn't keep her any longer. But she was making the work so much harder for me that it was hard enough before. And then I couldn't lift the chase. Well, dumbly it didn't occur to me to have the chase put on the press and kept there. Mr. Johns hadn't done it that way. He put everything in it on the stone and then carried it to the press and then put it back on the stone. And it didn't occur to me that that chase could just sit on the press all the time and I could fill it there as well as anywhere. So there was, well, he was one of the old sons in Troy, which one I can't tell you now, who would come in on press day and take the chase to the press and later take it off and bring it back to the stone for me. So we got along.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Your correspondence, did you have regular correspondence?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: I had one or possibly two, I'm not sure which, regular outside correspondence.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Ethel and I both kept our ears wide for news all of the time and people just came and brought it to me because they were so happy to know what the news was. And I did enjoy it very much, but Ethel was graduating this second year, ready for university. And we had decided that we would go on with the paper and get a car and Ethel would drive from Troy to the university in good weather or live in a hall through the winter.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: But there began to be opposition to a woman owning any business.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And, well, I wasn't hard enough. I've grown harder since, I hope. I wasn't hard enough to take it and tell them, well, object if you want to, I don't care. So the opposition was appointed that I sold when this man came along and offered me so much more than the place was worth.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: On what basis could they be opposed to a woman? On what basis could these people be opposed to a woman owning a newspaper?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: They were all foreign extract. It was common saying that the postmaster and his family and myself were the only Americans in there. It was the foreign element where women do not. At that time, you see, this was 48 years ago. That's quite a little while. And a great many of these people themselves had come across. Not descendants, but of themselves. And it was just simply this foreign idea.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In Europe, in their old countries, you were starting to say the women didn't have that kind of authority? Is that the case?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: No, a woman didn't own business. A woman didn't own a business. She owned her husband's name. That's not only in the foreign speaking people. There was here in Moscow a woman whom I well knew. Her husband was English. And she said to me one time, she'd been asking me if I thought she should do this, that, or the other. And I said yes. And she said, well, you know how it is. The English do not expect their women to have the liberty that women in the United States have. So it isn't only the foreign speaking people who objected to women owning business. But according to her, an Englishman has an idea that a woman owns her husband's name. And that is sufficient. Maybe it is.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that the women in Troy supported your being the editor more than the men did?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: I think that the common people did. The trouble did not come from the common people. It came higher up than that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The prosperous ones like the farmers and the businessmen?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Well, I think some of the businessmen did not object. I know I had one item about a woman who was actually the head in Troy of a business that was part of a business in Moscow at the time. It was the outlying station. And this woman was the business. There was no question on it. And I had a nice little write up for her. Something about a woman doing this work and so capably. And a relative of hers came in and he did read me the riot act for writing that article. He said she is not the head of that. Well, she was and there was no denying it. But anyway, this man who was related to her wasn't going to have anybody say that any woman could run a business.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How deep seated do you think this idea was that women were supposed to be in the home and be sort of subservient?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Well, I don't know where the idea came from but I think it was always there.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: To me, this idea of trying to decide which is greater, male or female, is just exactly as though the steering wheel on an automobile. And one of the wheels under the automobile got into a discussion and each of them claimed to be the greater. And the steering wheel said to the wheel with the tire on it, I'd like to know how you'd get where you want to go if it wasn't for me up here guiding you. And the wheel with the tire comes right back.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Well, how would you get there in spite of all the steering you do if I didn't run?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: So for one to say it's any greater than the other is nonsense. Each one in its place is it. And that's all there is to it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where you were, were you in such a place to see the struggle for the right to vote for women? Did that come about?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Yes, yes. I remember distinctly about that time.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: There really was quite a struggle about women getting to vote. But they persevered and all the women's organizations took it up and finally carried it over. And I remember one campaign speech that I heard when the vote was newer than it is now, a good deal. And this woman was talking about the candidate having a rainbow around his shoulders. And I thought, well, that's a funny campaign talk. So I think the women were sort of new at going at the business of voting and talking and so on.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: I remember quite a number of things.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: I remember when William Jennings Bryan was so much in the public eye. My father,

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: what I was going to say sort of slipped from me. But anyway, he was a politician first. And he was father afterwards. There was no time left, but usually there wasn't much time left after the politics. So I guess I come up with my political activities, honestly.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were your father's politics?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: He was straight Republican. There wasn't anything else on the face of the earth that was any good but a Republican. A Democrat was just dirt, that was all. We had the two parties for a while and we had various other parties. We had the Populist party. It happened that an uncle of mine was a Populist and he wasn't in our county, but in his county there was one Populist vote. And since he was a Populist, of course they know who had voted it. And someone was razzing him about it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He said, well, how do you feel now?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: He said, oh, I feel just like a good egg in a bushel of bad ones.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: The Populist party did make quite a mark for a while.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Your father, as a Republican, what did he think of the La Follette Republicans? What did he think of those progressive Republicans, the ones that wanted to reform the party?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Oh no, no reform was needed. They were perfect like they were. So he wasn't for the free silver and what Brian... I never heard him discuss silver. I do remember that I was at a gathering where an outside orator, and he was a real orator, was discussing the silver question. And he explained it that it was just a matter of 16 to 1. And very simple, and went on explaining it. And he says, if I had a boy of 16 who couldn't understand that, I'd whip him. Well, I wondered how that would make him understand it. But this was a real orator, and one of his remarks stuck with me. I was just a kid at the time. And he said they go around talking about prosperity, parity, parity. And he could put over little things like that just instantly and sweep on into other things with such ease that I just sat there and admired the way he could talk. And I heard... I wonder if I can remember her name as I talk. She was one of the greatest advocates of the vote for women. And she spoke from the rostrum in our courtroom. We were the county seat. She spoke there, and I went to hear her. And I admired... she was a large woman, tall and wide and thick. And I admired the way without a particle of self -consciousness she went up onto the rostrum and took her chair there.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And she took for her text, telling us just where in the Bible it is found, which I do not remember now. Let no man take thy crown. I've always remembered how she could bring out that word, man. But she gave a great talk, why women should vote.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember the arguments at all that she used in that talk? What her reasoning was?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: I do not remember accepting her giving the topic in the way she did. But at the time, I fully agreed with her that she was perfectly right about women having the vote.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where was this that you saw her? What was the town and the state?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Redfield, South Dakota.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did many speakers come through and speak to the people there?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: We had many speakers. We were a county seat town, and we were a very large town when I first knew it. I think there might have been 2 ,000 people there. And it grew and grew. And we had what we called Redfield College. It was a denominational college of the congregational church. And the campus consisted of one three -story red brick building with a customary path, of course. And the basement at first was just merely a storage place, but very soon it was made into a kitchen and dining room, and a cook was employed. The men students, well that was the basement. Then the first floor was the classrooms. The second floor was the dormitory for the men students. The third floor was the dormitory for the women students. Well, a women's organization in the town took it up that that was not the proper way to lodge students. That there had to be a separate building for a dormitory for the women students. So about a block from the one building that we had at first, they put up a two -story building with a bedroom and a sitting room for the house mother. And I think it was two extra bedrooms. And the second floor had bedrooms, what it would hold. And that was sufficient for the women who had no other lodging. There was one student there that I always remember very distinctly. She had been to the college building, or near it, and then she came to our home, which was a block from the college building. And Mother took her in and she was inquiring about how to get connected with the college. And Mother said, well, Reverend Beaton is the president and Clara will take you over there to Reverend Beaton and he will tell you all these things that you want to know. So Clara listened carefully to her conversation with Mother so that her ears didn't miss anything. And then she took the girl across town, clear to the other side, where the president, Reverend Beaton and his family lived.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And when we got near to it, Mary Hart said, well, where is the house? Which one is it?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And I pointed and I said, that's the one. And she said, well, then you can go home now. Well, I didn't want to go home now. I wanted to take her right to the door. I wanted to know everything I could know. So I kept going and she said, you can go home now. And when we got somewhere near it, she said, you'd better go home now. Well, I took the hint finally and went home. And she went on to see Reverend Beaton. Well, she made out some way. I found out later that she had given out at her home on a farm near Aberdeen, which was 40 miles or so north of us. That she was going to college and her father told her she couldn't, that he had no money for her to go with. She said she was going anyway. She was going to college and nothing was going to stop her. So when she was ready to go to college, he gave her a $10 bill. And he said, now Mary, don't ask me for anything more. I cannot give you another penny and pay the taxes too. He said, that is all that I can give you for the whole year. So with her $10 bill, she got herself to Redfield. And I don't know just where she lived or how she got by that year, but she did. And she put in her four years there, her brother joining her for the two last years. And when the brother was with her, they got a room over the drugstore. It was a drugstore and oh, sort of a general store in a way. And up above, there were two rooms that had a stove and a couple of chairs. And she and her brother lived up there and they lived practically without heat through the whole university and college year. It wasn't a university, it was a college. And for their supper, they would build just fire enough that she could cook some supper. And when that fire was gone, there was no more fire until the next supper. And she and her brother on the two kitchen chairs would sit at the table with their books and one blanket around the two of them so they could keep a little warmer. And there they did their work for the next day. And she took all that and living entirely without money and it was just terrible how destitute she was, but she graduated anyway. And I don't know what became of her and her brother, but I do know that she graduated. And we were all proud of her.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Did you see her during those years?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: I was the way I was known.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did she visit with the family? Is that how you saw her? Or did you used to go over to the college to see her? Where would you run into her during those school years? Where was I? Where did you run into her? Did she come to the house and visit the family?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: She had come to our house and, well, we lived only a block from the college. And at one time we lived a block in front of it and then we had our own home, a block sort of to one side of it. But going to and from town, the students passed by our door as we lived in front of the building. And I'd see her, she'd go back and forth and sometimes I'd meet her here and there. Never had any close connection with her. You said a while ago that the women's organization wanted the building of a new dormitory for the girls.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I'm wondering, were the women's organizations very active when you were young?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Yes, they were. There weren't so many of them by any means. There was the ladies' aid of the churches. That was one group. And that was it for the women, was the ladies' aid. But it was very active. I do not recall any name of any other women's organization.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the Women's Christian Temperance Union?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: The Women's Christian Temperance Union was organized by Frances Willard in 1876. Next year is our centennial year. My grandmother, Connor, was one of the first members of that group. My mother was a member of that group. I am a member of that group and have been district president for several years and for two years was state organizer with national recognition to work across the border in Washington. That happened because as I was driving from one place to another in Idaho, naturally, because the highway ran on both sides of the state line, I was sometimes in Washington and a group there would ask me for help there. So I wrote to national and explained it to them and they gave me recognition to work in Washington as well as in Idaho.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Your grandmother was one of the earliest people to become a part of that group?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: She was one of the earliest of the members of the Women's Christian Temperance Union.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did she ever speak to you about it? Did you know why she grew to want to join it?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: It's very plain to me that you don't know the old -time pioneer women. They didn't speak to.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: They were quite a silent group. Everything was scarce with them, even words. No, my grandmother didn't talk with me. I can't remember any time that she ever really talked with me. I did her housework for her for several months one time. But she would speak to me if necessary, but she didn't talk with me.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: My grandfather,

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: well how he came to be my grandfather, he was the beau of his community way back when. And grandmother was the beau of her community. My grandfather heard about that beau of Sally Lilly. He'd go over and break her heart and go back home. My grandmother heard of that beau of Elias Conner. He didn't need to come seeing her. So Elias left his community and rode his horse over to Sally's community.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Six weeks later they were married.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Their first morning in their new home, grandfather went out to take care of his saddle horse. And grandmother was going to show him how lively she was, how she could get around and do things. She'd have breakfast ready when he got that horse taken care of. So she fried a skillet of eggs on the fireplace, which was the only way of having fire they had. She tried a skillet of eggs and pulled it back onto the hearth of the fireplace and turned around and stepped in it. Her children never got over teasing her about that. But anyway, they lived together for more than 50 years. And as usual, she got up very early in the morning, got the breakfast, washed it up, got the dinner, washed it up, got the supper and washed it up. Went to bed and died instantly. After I think it was about 54 years they were together. And grandfather expressed himself this way, I have a good home. I have one of my children still with me, but I am utterly bankrupt. That was his way of expressing his thought for the death of his wife.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: It sounds like they must have had a very good marriage. They had a good marriage. And as far as the Women's Christian Temperance Union is concerned, he drank quite a bit when he was young. And when some of his children were teenagers, there was an evangelist come to their community in Minnesota. And grandfather and grandmother and some of the children were thoroughly converted from a life they'd had to a Christian life. And grandfather never drank another drop in his life. And none of his children drank. And grandmother was all for everything that could be done to fight the liquor traffic. She made her mark in her community.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And by nature, she was not a mother. She was never intended for a mother. But she was the most marvelous gardener that the world has ever known. And when they moved to South Dakota, it is said that the government report of the annual rainfall for that portion of South Dakota was 14 inches. Well, that's below anything that is supposed to be able to raise a crop. But have a garden, this woman would. The very first thing she did was to begin to pick up stone. And she didn't have to look anywhere for them. They were all over, everywhere. She picked up stone and built a stone wall, oh, more than two, probably nearly three feet high, around a plot right by the house for her garden. Well, when I knew it, I wasn't even in South Dakota, not South Dakota, D -A -C -O -T -A -H Dakota Territory, when we went there. But she built a stone wall around this garden plot. And at the time that I knew it, in the fall, she would have several inches deep of litter from the cattle barn spread all over her garden. And left there until spring, the garden was plowed as deep as possible, getting this litter mixed into the soil. Then after it was plowed with two horse plow, as deep as possible, she had it raked and raked and raked with a horse rake. They went over and over and over that with the horse rake. Then, when she would finally let the horse rake go, she took the hand rake, and she hand raked every inch of it. And only then could seeds be put in the ground. But when that little old lady put a seed in the ground and said, now you grow, it grew, and it produced. She had in that dry country, and they had no water except what was hauled in a barrel. So you can be sure she didn't water her garden. And she didn't raise plants in the house because there was no place in the house to put a plant, for one thing. But she planted the seeds right in the ground, and she had tomatoes and watermelons and muskmelon. They were not cantaloupe at that time, they were muskmelon. And finally they degraded into cantaloupe. And asparagus.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And she would plant a few potatoes just for early new potatoes.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And she had corn aplenty. And lettuce and radish. She had one radish that was known as a winter radish, and it got to be about six inches long, and well about half an inch in diameter. And it was white. And she took great pride in that harvest time, having those radishes on the table for the harvesters. And they were good and sweet and tender at that time of year. And I had the seed of them at one time, but I finally lost it.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: I'm talking about that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: May I just ask you one thing here?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Gardening, I take it gardening was her pride and joy. It was especially important. It was her pride and her joy was gardening. And there wasn't anything that anybody could tell her knew about gardening that she didn't already know. And she had currant bushes that were just loaded, and she sold the currants, and she sold her garden stuff too, so that she had a little supply of cash of her own all the time. She would, Uncle Will would hitch old Kate to the buggy. Kate couldn't run away if she wanted to, and Grandmother would hold the lines. I wouldn't say she drove, but she held the lines. And old Kate took her to town and around the little streets, and she sold her gardenware that way. And she had just about everything that is raised in a garden.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this a lifelong interest of hers, or one that she developed in her later years?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: I think she had been born with a hole and rake in her hands. And she had gardened before she went to South Dakota. She had gardened in Minnesota. And she had one hole that she used for more than 50 years. And it was worn down until the blade was only half the usual width. But the thing she could do with that hole, she would get up at 4 o 'clock in the morning, and go right out to her garden and start hoeing. And when she would get a hoe from this corner to that, she would come right back and start again at this corner. She hoeed continually. She said there was water in the ground, she was going to have it up where her plants could get it, and she did. She proved her theory. And her gardening ability showed up in many of her descendants. Many of them were great gardeners. I'm not a great gardener, I never was, but I do know an experiment that possibly, if our population continues to increase so rapidly, and if the adverse elements continue taking land away from the common people into government hands, this thing that I know may be a real life saver. It is this. Up near Everett, Washington, a little town there, a man was given what the one who gave it to him said was a separate kind of potato, just Irish potato, that you plant deep in the ground and keep covering it until you get to ground level with the cover, and at each joint it will put out a crop of potatoes. Well this man planted the potato, oh, perhaps a foot and a half or even deeper in a hole in the ground, and at Everett, in that country up there, the soil is big woods loam, which never hardens like the clay. And he harvested almost one bushel of potatoes from that one little hole in the ground. Well I had that seed, and I had it for years, until I had ten years of illness. The husk did quite a lot to me, and during that time I lost the seed of it. But, when I was able again to do some gardening, I planted a common potato, and it performed in the same way. A year ago, I had a student gardening my ground here in Moscow, and I planted just a common potato in a hole that he dug,

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: and I was going to keep track exactly of the produce from it, but lo and behold, he, an Ag major, senior, without my consent or knowledge, just to be good to me, I continued digging time, went out and dug that up and never kept any record at all of anything about it. An Ag student, a senior. So I don't know just how it performed, excepting that when I happened to go out and find what he was doing, and he was almost through with it, he had on the ground about four good sized potatoes, and a large number of very small potatoes of various small sizes. They were really small, and it was so many, I was so furious at him doing this, although he had meant it for kindness, that I wouldn't ask him anything about it, I wouldn't speak to him about it. But he had so many of these small ones that it looked to me as though they could not have come from one joint. They must have been from more than one joint, because there certainly were twenty or more of them. My idea is that if we...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did you yourself become interested in the WCTU and become an active member?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Oh, I became a member of the WCTU after I came to Moscow.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And it is a fact that I didn't join when I was first asked to join, because the dues were a dollar, and I didn't have quite dollars enough to feed myself and my daughter, as we should have been fed. So I waited two or three years until I had a dollar I could spare for the dues. And that was... It's more than forty years ago. I don't remember what year it was, but I've been here forty -eight years. And I think it's about forty -two or three years ago, perhaps, that I joined.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And I have now given up most of the activities of it, excepting that I have charge of the devotions at every meeting. Not because I want to give up, but ninety -six years makes you give up quite a number of things, whether you will or not.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And your mother was active in the WCTU before you?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: My mother was a jail visitor in Redfield, South Dakota. And for years I was a jail visitor here in Moscow. And I confined my jail visiting to the juveniles. There was one juvenile, I wouldn't mention his name if I did remember it, names and faces have no meaning for me anymore. They don't register. I remember the person, all the incidents, but the name and the face don't register. In some ways that's the least important part anyway. But there was this one boy who was a perfectly good boy, but a bunch, a group, and this was a blood -sworn group of boys.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Can you feature it?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Just young boys in grade school being blood-sworn, every one of them had had his wrists slit for a blood token of his loyalty to the group. Well this boy was not that kind of a boy at all, either by raising or by nature, but they needed a stool pigeon. And they got him in some way or other, and he didn't know how to get out. And when it came to a matter of the officers, lo and behold here's this stool pigeon to put up. And I visited him in jail. And the sheriff was very considerate. He took me and the boy into his private office and let us talk there. Well the boy was, in spite of the fact that he nearly bit his tongue out trying to avoid it, he was crying bitterly. His idea was that everything for him was over. There was no future. There was nothing. Well I talked to him and showed him that there was a future, that he could recover from this. And finally got him comforted and quieted. And I kept track of him the while that he was in the jail. During this time I was cooking on the campus, but anyway I found time to visit this boy in the evening. And at that time the sheriff would let a jail visitor in at any time that she could be there. So there was no question on that. And when I'd get home in the evening I'd go and visit this boy every now and then. He was in the jail a while. And they sent him to the reform school.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he actually commit a crime?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: He was with the boys who did. And how much he did of it I do not know. But he was with the boys.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think he came to trust you? Do you think he grew to trust you as someone who wanted to help him?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Well,

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: he was just in this position that he was jailed and they had to keep him. And I'm sure that the sheriff himself saw the proposition as it was that he was merely the stool pigeon who had been taken in for the purpose of being taken. And he was with the boys when they committed whatever it was. I do not remember. But whether he actually was a commitment I do not know. I didn't try to find out. But anyway I wrote to him all the while that he was in the home. And as soon as he came back to Moscow, discharged from the home, he had a good home here. He had a good Christian mother. He'd been well raised. As soon as he came he came to see me. And I kept track of him. Well, as years went on he grew up and married a good girl, had a nice family.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: He brought me home from a church nursery that I had charge of. He brought me home from there one day with his son. I don't remember perhaps he had two children with him. I know he had a son with him as he brought me home. And the son was a lively rascal. And he wanted to know if I knew his father. And I said yes, I knew his father. Well, I could see instantly that the father's record was not known to the children and that he was afraid it would leak out now. Well, I took a lot of care that it didn't leak out. It was his business, not mine, whether this record was known to the children. He was just on nettles for a minute for fear of an answer that I would make to the boy. But I made a roundabout answer that quieted the boy and said nothing. It sounds as though he appreciated you for helping him then. He did. He was very appreciative. And there was another boy who stole oil or gas or something in Troy. And he was sent to the home. I visited him while he was in the jail here. And I wrote to him all the time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And one time I said, now is there anything you want that I could send you?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And he replied, yes, I'm building a chest and I want you to send me a lock that nobody can open.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Evidently he didn't remember that he'd opened a lock. Because he had opened a lock to get in. But sometimes you get a little fond along with all these things you do.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there this position of jail walker? This was a responsibility that the WCTU had a person take on?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: It's a separate department. We have a lot of departments of work. And this was one department and the jail visitor carried it on in her own way. On one of my organizing trips in South Idaho, I went to a Sunday morning service at the jail with the jail visitor there. And every Sunday morning at this hour, she went to the jail and the sheriff had the men all congregated where she could stand in the hall with everything between her and the men. The men were not given any liberty at all. They were confined, but they were all in one place and she could stand in the hall outside. And she sang with them and prayed with them and talked with them. And that was her regular Sunday morning service as jail visitor. And each one carries on the department in her own way. My way was to visit them personally. I visited one boy in the jail here. There was something wrong with his mentality.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: To destroy and steal and kill was the only thing he knew anything about. But anyway, I went to visit him and the sheriff said that he really was dangerous. But if I wanted to, I might talk with him. I said yes, I would like to talk with him. So the sheriff took me upstairs to the boy's cell and put me in the cell and locked the door. And I had just gotten the boy from his braggadocio mode to where he could make some response to what I was saying to him. When the jailer got worried about it because the time had gone long and he came up to see if I was still alive or not. He said when they took the boy he was carrying a six -shooter. And he was just a kid.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: But anyway, I didn't go back to see this boy because apparently there was nothing in the brief time that he would be here that anybody could have done that would have been lasting with him. He was just mentally down.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: But we do have... One woman said to me the other day, she said, well I don't care so much about your WCTU. All you ever do is justify it booze. I said, no, I don't believe it is. I said it happens that the woman who created the WCTU was responsible for the eight -hour law and for the child labor law. I said we do quite a lot of things besides fight booze, although we do fight it tooth and toenail all of the time. But we do have all these departments. And at present, whether they have found the person or not I don't know, but we are looking, if they haven't already found it, we are looking for a family. A whole family that absolutely cannot buy a real Thanksgiving dinner so that we may send them a dinner. We do that each year. Last year we sent two dinners to two families. And you don't know how difficult it is when you want to give somebody something to find the person who ought to have it. It's just like finding a needle in a haystack. Last year we couldn't find anybody that fitted the classification of someone who simply could not buy a dinner. But we were given two names. One of them was a family where the mother worked. The father was invalid. And while they got along, it was just to the end of everything, every paycheck. And the other one was a woman whose husband was incarcerated, either in Idaho or somewhere, presumably in Boise.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And they were not only welfare cared for, but each year the lodges have their Thanksgiving dinners that they give. You probably know about that. They were to get one of those dinners.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: So the president said to me, well, what will we do?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Well, I said we were given the name. Let's give her the dinner anyway. And this other one that you mentioned, I said since they are sort of provided for but just on a shoestring basis, I said let's give them a dinner too. So we gave two dinners last year, which is unusual.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it easier in the early days, was the suffering or the want of families much more obvious than it is today? Was it more apparent? Well, in the early days, if you were looking for families to help, were they easier to find?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: No, they never have been easier to find. For instance, there was a family, well, it's over right on the border of the campus where they were living, Railroad Street somewhere over there. And I said that this was at Christmas time. We were looking for someone because they had suggested to find a family with children and send toys for the children at Christmas, besides the Thanksgiving dinner, which we had done. And I said, I thought that we had no business giving just helter skelter, that every name that we got, that person should be visited and find out if they should receive help or if there was no use for it. Well, they said if that's the way you feel, go and do it. So I was going around visiting these people and I found one place where the father was a painter. And at that time, the painter, if you please, was getting the enormous wage of $8 a day. At that year, $8 was a lot of money. And he was getting $8 a day, but the Sloan was getting it. And we had been given that name. And when I went there, they had an apartment, a good -sized apartment. And there was a bay window, like an old house would have. And that bay window was fairly well filled with donations that had been given them. But anyway, there was a university professor and his wife who took me around to deliver the gift.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And they had some huge oranges, a special shipment of them at the store, those immense -sized oranges. And so they left oranges there, in spite of all that had been given them. Another place that I went to previously for the visit, I asked a woman about what they would need for the dinner, what they would like to have besides the turkey. Why, she said, I don't want a dinner given me. I said, well, we were given your name. Well, but she said, before my husband's death, he had made provision for me that I don't need anything. I have everything that myself and the children need because my husband provided it. Her husband's death had been very sudden and very recent in an accident of some kind. And I said, well, but you put your name in at a certain place that I designated. I don't know what the designation was. And she said, yes, but I thought it merely meant some little treat of candy or something for the children.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: A woman said to me, why don't you put your name in?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: I put mine.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And why don't you put yours?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: So I put my name in just thinking it would be something that would help to make the day for the children. But she said, I will not take a dinner. I wouldn't think of it. Well,

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: that place was providing dinners. That's what they were for, was dinners. But she had thought differently. So that one was out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about this family that got all the other food that was getting so much that you just told me about? Did you give them a dinner?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: No, no, we weren't giving dinners for Christmas. We were just giving a treat. And we had, I think it was two little Christmas trees, one anyway. But anyway, there was another place that had been given me. Well, when I was half a, I was in the car with this university man and his wife. And when we were half a block from the place, we knew it by the odor. Oh, it was terrible.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Well, I hadn't visited them before. I was just simply taking the treats. So I did the best I could about the odor and went to the door. Well, it was very plain to me what the place was for. I didn't know whether the man at the back door had just come in and was interrupted by my knocking at the front door or whether he was just leaving. But I found out that it was a place that was being maintained.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Well, what will I say? The upper class men who wouldn't go there?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: I don't think there is any such thing as upper class and lower class. I think we're all people. But still to express it, it was a place kept for other than upper class. And I found out later, not then, but later, I found out that the mother who had just died was getting welfare for herself and the family. That the son had a full -time job. He was a moron. But he had a full -time job at something or other and was getting his full wage. And then the daughter was making this extra money. And yet, we were asked to take them a Christmas treat. Well, there were two little boys. The woman didn't make any pretense at all that she'd ever been married, but she had two little boys. And it looked to me like they were both of at least first grade school age. They were playing in the backyard. It was a nice day. And I asked her if she would like to have a Christmas tree, thinking of the boys. She said I wouldn't have anything to put on it if I did have one. And I said, well, I think the boys would like it. And I said, we have. There's some things to put on it if you'll let us come and put it up. So she said yes, put it up if we wanted to. So we did with the things to put on it. And that's the kind of experiences we have.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you say odor, by the way, did it just stink? Was that it?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: You said the house really smelled from a half a block away. It was just their way of living. That was all. They had been there for years in that one spot. And I just can't describe it. It was sort of a one room place with some of it curtained off. And the moron son was standing there grinning at me.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And the woman was really much embarrassed. But she didn't get up from her cot where she was lying. The house itself was probably filthy. It was absolutely filthy. And the yard was filthy. And although other places were not rain wet, this place seemed to be. I don't know whether they threw out their dishwater or what. But anyway, the ground was wet around it. There was a plank from the street to the door. I never have seen anything quite as terrible as that was. And I complained so loudly to the health officer. Dr. Litch was health officer at that time and I knew him. And I just laid it on and complained to him. And no, he was health officer at one time. Somebody else I think. Somebody else was at that time. But anyway, I didn't spare anything to tell him about it. And not long after, the family went to California.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And someone said to me, well, you didn't do anything for them, did you?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: I said, well, at least I did enough for the town that they're gone. Yes, but somebody else will have them. I said, well, it will take them some years to get another place to be like the one they left. And at least where they were can be cleaned up. I think that making the move was a perfectly right thing to do, even if somebody else did get them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about times when there were people who were in need, who were in very desperate need? Do you remember situations like that?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: I've seen way back in Redfield when I was a kid. South Dakota, Redfield, South Dakota. Mother fed every man who came to the door. There was a string on them. Sometimes it would be a businessman in his business suit. Sometimes it would be some bedraggled tramp in his Levi's. Although they weren't Levi's at that time, they were overalls. But Mother fed everyone who came. Someone said to me one time, well, your mother has no business to do that. She'll be found dead there by one of those fellows some of these days. Well, I ran home as hard as I could go and told Mother. And Mother said, well, there is no man as dangerous as a hungry man. And she went right on feeding them. But it really was pitiful. When I was, it was hard times after that. I was temporarily, just for a short time, in Des Moines, Iowa.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And one day when the landlady was gone, and I tended the door for her when she was gone, the man came to the door. He was just a common businessman in his business suit. It was very apparent that he was not a tramp in any way whatever. And he asked me if he could have something to eat. Well, myself and a little girl in an apartment just for a little short time. But I found something for him anyway.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And he said to me, do you have any coffee?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: I said, no, I never drink it. I don't have any in the house. Oh, he was so disappointed. But that was another hard time years later than this other one. And at one time later than that, I was at Heath, Montana, in the country.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And he had evidently lost his means in something about a picture machine. Well, at that time, a picture machine was a very new thing. The movies. It was very new. And he would mumble to himself about a picture machine. Well, it was plain that his mind was turned. Well, here I was, alone with a little child in the country. I didn't know whether he was going to go or not. But finally he did go on.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: But a person meets all kinds.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you have much of an idea of what caused businessmen or people who had some means to lose what they had? Was it just hard times in the country? What happened?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: The banks were all closed with all that the person had in the bank. He had just what he had in his pocket. Just one bank after another went out. And it was real hard times. At your age, you've never seen real hard times. But there were people who had been living in comfort. That when they ate what was in the house, they were out.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: I remember one time, we were living on a farm at Geyser, Montana. And there were two men who came along. We were seven miles from town. I don't know.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Maybe they came out with my husband when he drove out from town. Anyway, they were there to suffer. My husband gave them a blanket and they slept in the manger. One of them was a tramp. I don't see tramps anymore. We used to have them. They were known as tramps and they were tramps. They had no other destination than some place that isn't here. And they had that all of the time. And they lived on what they could get back doors. But anyway, these two men were there at our ranch and they came in and sat to suffer with us. And then they had this blanket and slept in the manger and they came into breakfast and then they went on. One of them was thoroughly a tramp. And the other was absolutely an educated businessman.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And I marveled at the combination and why this businessman was out like this. It is possible that he was running from the law. He may have been in on some crooked deal. I don't know. That was as near as I could get at any guess that seemed reasonable. But anyway, the two men were so different. But when they had their breakfast, they just wandered on. But why they would be here, seven miles from town. So I thought probably the man might have been running from the law.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you were young and your mother was feeding the men that came to the door, was it common for someone to come to the door? Knock on the door and say, I'm hungry?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Just very common. Just very common. There was just one after another. And sometimes they would, oh, if there was a piece of wood to be cut up, maybe they'd cut that up or something like that. But usually they were just fed and that was all. And mother always had coffee for them and of course that was a treat.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: No, it was so common that we thought nothing of it at all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would this be during one very bad year or two bad years or did it go on year after year?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: No, it was I think not more than two years anyway. It might have been even shorter, I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Perhaps a depression going on in the country?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: The Depression was in general, the whole United States. It was general. That was about 1890.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: I had that as a pivotal date because my baby brother was born in 1890. So I had the date. And he was, I think he was about a year old at this time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were most of these men just traveling around from town to town?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: No. Just going. Of course the tramps were finding it harder because there were so many cutting in on them to get handouts. And perhaps someplace wouldn't give so many handouts, is all that. I had one odd circumstance right here on this doorstep and I've wondered about it ever since. I happened to be looking out, it was summertime, and a big long room tall man carrying a sack in his hand was coming up the drive. And I wondered who or what. So I stood looking out the window and he went to the steps that go up the bank in front of the garage. And he looked up there quite a little bit and then he turned and went away. And after a while, here was the same man at the door. So I went to the door and asked him what it was. He said he wanted to know if there was a mission in this town.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: I said, well, but what kind of a mission?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: The kind that have dinners, that kind. I haven't eaten much for the last two days. And I said, no, there is no such mission here, but you sit right down here on the doorstep and I'll bring you something. Well, it happened that I had just a very little bread in the house.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And one person alone, one old woman, doesn't have a lot of food around like families do. But anyway, I took the bread and some butter and something else, what I could find, and told him that it was all the bread that I had in the house. And later he said to me, when I asked you, I wondered if you would have supplies in the house. Well, he very evidently was not the ordinary traveler. He was something more. And I took a little folding stool and went out and sat on the other end of the step and talked with him.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: After a little, I said, if I make coffee for you, will you drink it?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Well, you know some people drink it and some don't. And he said, if it wouldn't be too much trouble. I said, there's no trouble at all. So I took him a bowl full of coffee and he was very grateful for it. And we talked on and he had this sack that he was carrying and around his neck he had a strap with a bill, not a bill pole, but a roof case hanging around his neck. And finally my curiosity got the better of me and it came into the conversation that I could fit it in.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: I said, and what is in that?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: He said, that is some personal items. So we didn't say any more about it. And among other things, while we were talking, he said that at one time he owned his own business. Well, I didn't doubt that he was correct. And we had quite a conversation. And when he had gotten up, he said, I want to do something for you.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: He said, what can I do that will help you?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: I said, well, I have a little piece of ground there that's hard to spade. You could spade that if you would. Well, it was plain to me that he had never handled a spade. He didn't know what they were for. But he did the best he could at it. And I thought that he was gone. He had brought the shovel up and left it. And I thought he was gone. But he came to the screen, and there was a screen in the outer door at the time of summer. He came to the door, and he said, goodbye, Mrs. Grove.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Well, I was too overthrown to say, how do you know who I am?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And he left very quickly.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: How did he know who I am? Who was he?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: I would like to know.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: I thought it out from every angle. There was no newspaper, no envelope, no nothing with my name on it anywhere. So he didn't get it here.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Well, where did he get it? How did he know it?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Evidently, I had known him somewhere. Perhaps back in school. I don't know.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: But he had come to those steps and gone away, and then he had come back. He was that determined to find me. There are things that are just impossible to explain. I don't know the explanation either. I've wondered and wondered about that. There was one old claim that disappeared. But when I compared the two, I didn't see how it could be, because there wasn't a physical resemblance.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: But... So I never have known, and I have often wondered, who he was. And when I bought this place,

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: I had no fitting of any kind for shifting for myself. And I wasn't making a very good out at it. Well, I was offered this place for a small sum, naturally. They were going to pave right away. Well, it didn't occur to me that you inquired about anything like that before you bought. But anyway, it was a small enough price that I could pay it right down and be done with it. And there were just those two rooms. Nothing more. The water was in, but it wasn't out. And from right here to the street, it was alfalfa about this high. Oh, it was a wonderful crop of alfalfa. Well, we got along with the two rooms for a little, until we could kind of get settled and get our breath and find where the university was and one thing and another. And then I had a little shed room built right on there from that door for a bedroom. And that was there for years before I was so I could have this part built on. One of my brothers built this in 1940. You see, I had come in 1927 and it was partly before I had got financially fixed that I could have this built. And in that year, and for, I think it was at least two years later, carpenters were working for 45 cents an hour. I have to this day some covered shelves and doors that a man, he was the best carpenter I think had ever been turned out. And when he got through, he was a very rapid worker, when he got through I asked him how much I owed him and he said 67 cents. And he had put up two sets of shelves with doors.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Well, I gave him three quarters. And he said, but I have no change with me. I thought yes and you don't have any at home either. So I said, well never mind, give it to the children for candy. Well, I knew it wasn't going to anybody for candy. But anyway, he accepted the three quarters. I wasn't so awfully flush myself. I was feeding college men. And while I had a profit every month, it wasn't big by any means. But you know that time that I had those college men is one of the highest lights of my life. And there's one of them from whom I hear a real folio every Christmas telling about the whole, you may run across his name sometime around this country, Paul Rice.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: He was getting his masters at the time that he ate here for two years. And later from an Eastern University, I don't know where, he got his doctor's degree. And he was an entomologist with Uncle Sam. And Uncle Sam sent him to,

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: oh it was a place that was in all the papers on my brewtongue at the time, across the waters. Sent him there. And he wrote to me every now and then. And even yet, after all these years, every Christmas I get a whole folio about the whole family.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you say that it was one of the highest lights in your life, was that because of the young men were looking for a home away from home? Or why was it such a special occasion?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Well, it was the caliber of the young men and their suitability to each other. I had two frosh here a while. And I wasn't making any profit on those two frosh. They were farm boys, both of them. It took about as much to feed the two as it did the others, all the others. So one day one of them came in and he hated to say it. And I was just tickled to pieces about it. He said, Mrs. Grove, we found a place where we can get lodging and board and laundry for $15 a month. I said, well, Wayne, if I knew a place like that, I'd go on take it and shut this up.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: So the two of them left and I was so delighted. That left these older men.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they lodging here as well as boarding?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: No, no. They just ate, just the same as at a restaurant. That was all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But they came every day?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: Three times a day, except Sunday they came twice.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: They had an invitation that if they wanted anything later on Sunday, they could come in at any time and I'd get them something.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, there was one time that Paul came in and I said, what shall I fix for you, Paul? Well, he said, how about an egg?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: I said, just fine.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He said, do you think I could possibly make you see twice?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: I said, yes, I believe you could. And there was only one other time, and that was one of these sprochets at the time they were there here. They came in on Sunday evening. But otherwise... You served them all the same menu. They all sat at the table and ate family style. Well, I fed them rarely feed with plenty of it. They had meat every dinner and sometimes other times. But they had the best of meat every dinner. And we had always two vegetables at dinner instead of only one. And it was hot bread, home baked, frequently. Just exactly like home. And they were all so congenial. There was Floyd, who was teaching in the high school. And Paul, who was getting his master's. And Louie... I don't know whether he was getting his master's or not. I don't know.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And I don't know just who.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: I had six. That was the limit of what I should have.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Could you make a living from doing that? Was that enough for you to make a living?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: That was my living. That's all I had, was what I made out of that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And you could save from that too? To put aside?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: A little. Yes, I saved a little every month. Yes. And they often spoke about the marvel of getting so much food and the kind of food. And they were so congenial, they would discuss, you know, their math problems and all this, that and the other. Politics and everything under the sun, like men do when they're gathered together under congenial. And the last meal that they ate was evening dinner. They were very silent, looking at their plates. And finally, Floyd at the far end of the table lifted his head and he said, Well, there is one thing sure. We will never meet this again. There is no such thing as another bunch like this. That was their general opinion.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this the end of the school year?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: It was the end of the university year.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How much did you charge them for board then?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: I wonder if I know. It's not important. I was just wondering what it would have cost.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This was in the late twenties?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: It was in 32 and 33 and 4.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: I can't remember what I charged them. But I know I charged them in advance. I wasn't taking any chances on it at all. And more than that, I paid for the food as I bought it. And in that way I got the best of it. For instance, with the butcher, I never told him, I want chicken today. I want beef roast today.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: I always said, Well, what are we going to have today?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And he'd say, Well, I'm long on pork roast today. I said, That's fine. Send me one. And that's the way I bought my meat.

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And did I get the best of meat on the market?

speaker: Clara Payne Grove
words: And lots of it. I know there was finally one day he said, Well, I'm long on steak today. I said, Fine. We'll have steak. So we did. But he always chose the meat. And it was just as easy as any other way. And then I chose what to put with that meat. Let's see. I wish I could remember what I did charge you. Mrs. Grubaugh, I'm going to have to go live in one place. I matriculated at the university when I was 82. And of course I filled out the blank. And at the bottom of it, it said, Name the occupations you have followed. Well, I filled that line and I filled all the space below it. And I went up both sides. If there was anything I hadn't done, I don't know what it was. And I have had many adventures. I'd like to come back again and know in another week or so and take up some more. I'll have some things to ask, I'm sure. But I think it's very good to take this down on tape and save it for the county. Because in the future, when people want to know what these early days were like, I think your voice will be a good source to tell them. I know that your articles are good to tell them too. But it's different, you know.
